title: Update on Lear Community Meeting and Survey
resolved_text:
Memorandum
DATE: March 17, 2026
TO: Mayor and City Council
THROUGH: Jackie Bryant, City Manager
FROM: Megan Berner, Arts & Culture Manager
DEPT: Public Works
SUBJECT: Update on Lear Community Meeting and Survey
Background
In September 2025, Council directed staff to work with the Historical Resources Commission (HRC) and Dr. Alicia Barber to conduct community outreach regarding the Lear property and return with recommendations on next steps.
Community Engagement
• Community Meeting: January 10, 2026 at McKinley Arts & Culture Center, led by Dr. Alicia Barber, Councilmember Naomi Duerr (Ward 2 and Council Liaison to the HRC), and Arts & Culture staff.
• Attendance: 125 in person; 55 online via Zoom. The community meeting was recorded and posted on the City of Reno's website.
• Content: Lear property history, Historic Structure Report (HSR) findings, stabilization needs, and two conceptual use options.
• Survey: January 10–31, 2026; 441 responses, reflecting strong community interest.
• Community Priorities: Supporting local arts and culture; preserving historic architectural character; creating a public gathering space; inclusive programming; performing arts.
• Public Commission Review: Following the community meeting, public feedback was agendized and discussed at the January 15, 2026 HRC meeting, providing a second publicly noticed forum for engagement and deliberation.

HRC Advisory Recommendations

Based on community input and HRC commission discussion, the HRC voted to forward the following advisory recommendations to Council:
1. Prioritize Lear building stabilization and security, including fencing and exterior lighting.
2. Conduct a feasibility study addressing potential uses of the Lear property, market demand, operational and management models, and financing strategies.
3. Support fundraising efforts and allocate funding for Lear stabilization, including potential Redevelopment Agency participation through the budget process.
4. Establish an HRC advisory board or subcommittee for the Lear.
5. Continue community meetings to support ongoing public involvement.
Preliminary Cost Context (Planning-Level Only)
Provided for scale and context; not funding requests:
• Performing arts venue (theater seating): ~$22 million
• Flat-floor, flexible events space: ~$18 million
• Bringing the Lear building open and up to current code: ~$15 million
• Near-term stabilization and exterior improvements: ~$1.6 million, including short-term preventative maintenance (~$600,000), exterior concrete and railing repairs (~$600,000), fencing (~$200,000), and landscaping (~$200,000).

A feasibility study is recommended to refine costs, phasing, funding strategies, and operational models for the Lear property.
Risks & Considerations
• Delaying Lear stabilization may accelerate Lear deterioration, increase long-term costs, and raise safety and liability concerns.
• Historic designation and covenants will guide and limit allowable modifications to the Lear property.
• Long-term Lear operations are anticipated to require partnerships with nonprofit, community, and cultural organizations, to be evaluated through the feasibility study.
Next Steps / Council Action
• April 8, 2026: Council consideration of (1) authorization for near-term Lear stabilization and security improvements, (2) approval to proceed with a feasibility study, and (3) establishment of an HRC advisory board or subcommittee.
• Arts & Culture staff will continue community engagement and provide updates.

fields.tldr: Staff conducted a community meeting and survey in January 2026 regarding the historic Lear property, drawing 180 attendees and 441 survey responses. The Historical Resources Commission reviewed public feedback and forwarded five advisory recommendations to Council, including prioritizing stabilization, conducting a feasibility study, and establishing an HRC subcommittee. Planning-level cost estimates range from $1.6 million for near-term stabilization to $22 million for a full performing arts venue. Council is scheduled to consider authorization for stabilization work, a feasibility study, and an advisory subcommittee on April 8, 2026.
fields.key_stats: 125 in-person and 55 online attendees at January 10, 2026 community meeting; 441 survey responses (January 10–31, 2026); near-term stabilization estimate ~$1.6M; code-compliant building reopening ~$15M; flexible events space ~$18M; performing arts venue ~$22M.
fields.key_decisions: Council to consider on April 8, 2026: (1) authorization for near-term Lear stabilization and security improvements, (2) approval to proceed with a feasibility study, and (3) establishment of an HRC advisory board or subcommittee for the Lear property.
fields.action_items: April 8, 2026 Council meeting: authorize near-term stabilization/security improvements, approve feasibility study, and establish HRC advisory board/subcommittee; Arts & Culture staff to continue community engagement and provide updates; explore Redevelopment Agency participation in stabilization funding through budget process.
